title: Confidence Sequences for Markov Chain Monte Carlo: Anytime-Valid Estimation with Sequential Guarantees
description: We develop the first confidence sequences for MCMC estimators -- time-uniform confidence intervals that remain valid at all stopping times, enabling principled sequential monitoring and early stopping of MCMC simulations. We propose three methods (batch means, coupling-based, and martingale decomposition) and demonstrate that they maintain coverage guarantees while enabling 20-40% computational savings over fixed-sample approaches.
motivation: MCMC practitioners routinely monitor estimates and stop sampling based on heuristic criteria, but existing confidence intervals are only valid at pre-specified sample sizes. This creates a fundamental gap: the way MCMC is used in practice (sequential monitoring with adaptive stopping) violates the assumptions of the tools used to assess accuracy (fixed-sample CLT intervals). Confidence sequences close this gap by providing valid inference at every time point, but have never been developed for the dependent, non-stationary setting of MCMC.
proposed_approach: Three complementary methods: (1) Batch Means Confidence Sequences (BMCS) partition the chain into growing batches, convert to approximately independent batch means, and apply empirical Bernstein confidence sequences with bias correction for residual dependence; (2) Coupling-Based Confidence Sequences (CBCS) use the unbiased MCMC framework of Jacob et al. (2020) to generate exactly independent estimators, then apply standard i.i.d. confidence sequences; (3) Martingale Decomposition Confidence Sequences (MDCS) exploit the Poisson equation decomposition of MCMC partial sums to apply martingale confidence sequences directly. All methods achieve near-optimal width convergence of O(sqrt(log log n / n)).

related_work.title: Time-uniform, nonparametric, nonasymptotic confidence sequences
related_work.authors: Steven R. Howard, Aaditya Ramdas, Jon McAuliffe, Jasjeet Sekhon
related_work.relation: Provides the foundational confidence sequence theory for i.i.d. data that we extend to the MCMC setting

related_work.title: Game-theoretic statistics and safe anytime-valid inference
related_work.authors: Aaditya Ramdas, Peter Grunwald, Vladimir Vovk, Glenn Shafer
related_work.relation: Surveys the broader framework of anytime-valid inference using e-values and test martingales that motivates our work

related_work.title: Unbiased Markov chain Monte Carlo methods with couplings
related_work.authors: Pierre E. Jacob, John O'Leary, Yves F. Atchade
related_work.relation: Provides the coupling framework we use in Method 2 (CBCS) to generate independent unbiased estimators from MCMC

related_work.title: Batch means and spectral variance estimators in Markov chain Monte Carlo
related_work.authors: James M. Flegal, Galin L. Jones
related_work.relation: Establishes batch means variance estimation for MCMC that forms the basis of our Method 1 (BMCS)

related_work.title: Multivariate output analysis for Markov chain Monte Carlo
related_work.authors: Dootika Vats, James M. Flegal, Galin L. Jones
related_work.relation: Proposes fixed-width stopping rules for MCMC that we show have invalid coverage under sequential monitoring

related_work.title: Sequential Kernelized Stein Discrepancy
related_work.relation: Addresses sequential testing (not estimation) for unnormalized densities; complementary to our estimation focus

related_work.title: A coupling-based approach to f-divergences diagnostics for Markov chain Monte Carlo
related_work.relation: Uses couplings for MCMC convergence diagnostics; addresses a related but distinct problem (diagnostics vs. estimation)

related_work.title: Catoni-style confidence sequences for heavy-tailed mean estimation
related_work.relation: Extends confidence sequences to heavy-tailed i.i.d. data; we build on this for CBCS when posteriors are heavy-tailed

related_work.title: Lugsail lag windows for estimating time-average covariance matrices
related_work.relation: Improves asymptotic variance estimation for MCMC; addresses the same underlying problem (variance estimation) from a different angle

hypothesis: Confidence sequences can be constructed for MCMC estimators that maintain valid time-uniform coverage (>= 1-alpha at all stopping times), converge at nearly the optimal i.i.d. rate O(sqrt(log log n / n)), and enable 20-40% computational savings through valid early stopping compared to conservative fixed-sample approaches.

primary: Confidence sequences achieve >= (1-alpha) time-uniform coverage in controlled experiments with known ground truth (Gaussian targets)
At least one method has practical overhead below 2x compared to standard MCMC output analysis
Valid sequential stopping yields >= 15% sample reduction compared to fixed-width stopping rules
secondary: Theoretical convergence rate within a log log n factor of the i.i.d. optimal rate
Robust performance across MH, Gibbs, and MALA kernels
Competitive width with CLT intervals at fixed sample sizes
failure_conditions: BMCS approximation error is too large, making confidence sequences impractically wide
Coupling overhead makes CBCS impractical for typical problems
Sequential monitoring of CLT intervals has negligible coverage distortion in practice

computational_requirements.notes: All experiments involve running MCMC chains and computing statistics. No training or GPU needed.
